content:
5 min read
Just now

--

In my last post https://medium.com/@asad.eisa.dev/i-gave-my-ai-agents-amnesia-on-purpose-heres-why-it-made-them-better-dcc4a60a49b6 I explained *why* I built AKRS: agents drown in context, grade their own homework, and declare broken products “done.” AKRS routes the smallest correct knowledge to the correct agent at the correct moment — and a separate Tester checks the running product, not the paperwork.
This post is the how. Every step, every prompt, copy-paste ready. Total setup time: about 5 minutes, once per project.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Step 1 — Install it (5 seconds)
From inside your project:
npx akrs-framework initNo permanent dependency, nothing buried in `node_modules`. It copies the framework into `docs/akrs/`:
docs/akrs/
├── GETTING_STARTED.md ← the human on-ramp
└── framework/ ← the doctrine your planning model reads (01..11 + skills/)That’s the whole installation. These files are what your AI reads to learn how to build your project’s workflow.
Step 2 — Prepare your Source of Truth (the step everyone skips)
Your Source of Truth (SOT) is whatever describes what the project should be : requirements, architecture notes, the README of an existing repo — anything you’d hand a new senior engineer on day one.
Two rules that make everything downstream work:
Make it markdown, and make it sectioned. One file like `docs/app-info.md`, with real headings — `## Users`, `## Data model`, `## Screens`, `## Non-goals`. Routing works by pointing at sections a wall of text can’t be pointed at. If your knowledge lives in your head, spend twenty minutes brain-dumping it under headings. Ugly is fine. Sectioned is mandatory.
Confirmed is a fact; unconfirmed is an assumption. AKRS never starts on an assumption — the Leader will ask you to confirm which documents are authoritative before it builds anything. That confirmation is the foundation of the whole workflow.
Step 3 — Generate the workflow (once per project)
Open your strongest model — and turn its reasoning effort up: high, or extra-high if you have it. This thinking happens exactly once, and everything downstream rides on it. Don’t economize here; you’ll economize forever after.
Paste this:
Read the AKRS framework in docs/akrs/framework/.
My project's Source of Truth is: docs/app-info.md
Do this:
1. Confirm with me which documents are authoritative (Source of Truth).
Do NOT generate Tasks or Roads yet. Do NOT write application code yet.
2.init the workflow to meYou get back the skeleton — a compiled kernel/ (the only thing an execution agent reads to boot), a router, memory files, and `STATE.md`, the portable save-point. No tasks yet, deliberately: pre-built tasks rot the moment requirements change. AKRS generates them on demand, one at a time.
One check before moving on: make sure there’s an `AGENTS.md` at the project root (with thin pointers like `CLAUDE.md` for specific tools). It’s tiny — it just boots any agent into the kernel. If it’s missing, ask the Leader to create it per the platform-adapter spec. If your first Worker ever seems “lost,” 90% of the time it’s this file.
Step 4 — Get your first Road, then execute it
Ask the Leader for the first real piece of work: ( in new session )
you are leader : Generate the first Task and its Road .The Road names exactly which files to read, which files to change, and where the scope boundaries are. Skim it. If it does those three things, you’re ready.
Now the part that surprises people. Open a Worker — same model, a cheaper one, or a subagent, your choice — and give it the entire prompt:
Implement the active Road.That’s it. Don’t re-explain the project. The workflow already contains everything: the Worker boots `AGENTS.md → kernel → role file`, finds the active Road from `STATE.md`, reads only what the Road names, builds inside the boundaries, and stops. If it starts asking “what should I read?” — that’s a routing failure, not a model failure. Fix the Road, not the prompt.
Step 5 — Let the Tester check the running product
When the work touched anything a user sees, run a Tester — a fresh session, never the Worker’s:
You are Tester.
Verify the active Road against the RUNNING product - launch it and use it.
Never trust the transcript or the test suite's word for it.
You may not modify product code. Report PASS/FAIL with evidence, then stop.The Tester is structurally forbidden from touching code — it can only report and escalate. It exists because an agent grading its own homework will pass itself; mine once shipped a “done” feature running at 0.4 FPS with all 72 tests green. Since v1.3 the Tester doesn’t run per-road anymore: once per UI-touching plan, plus one final end-to-end pass.
Step 6 — Close-out is one line now
When work lands, the Worker flips the Road’s status to DONE and appends a single ledger line:
2026–07–05 · R07 · DONE ·
No essays. The Road is the record of what was built; the ledger is the receipt. Then verify mechanically:
npm run validate(Generated projects ship with the validate scripts wired in since v1.3.) It checks Road statuses, expected files, dependency gating, state budgets, ledger discipline — and exits non-zero on failure, so CI green = workflow valid.
When to skip the whole machine: Mode 0
AKRS isn’t a cage — the first thing it does with any prompt is picking a mode, and small work takes a fast path. Mode 0 is for when you already know where the problem is.
Real example. Say a price is displaying as `$1,00.00` and you already know it’s the formatter in `src/utils/money.ts`:
use Mode 0: fix the thousands-separator bug in src/utils/money.ts.
Load memory/formatting.md only. Nothing else.No router, no new Task, no Road — the Worker gets the one memory file that owns the relevant contracts plus the file you named, makes the fix, and the ledger gets its one line. A one-file fix costs one-file context. You pay for the full funnel only when the work deserves it.
Repo: (https://github.com/asadeisa/akrs) · install: `npx akrs-framework init` — MIT, zero dependencies. If this saved you a drowned agent, a star helps the project get found.
